The **2020 [World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships")** were scheduled to be held in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), from 16 to 22 March 2020.[[1]](#cite_note-announcement-1) Figure skaters would have competed for the title of world champion in [men's singles, ladies' singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pairs](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"). This would have been the first time that Montreal hosted the World Figure Skating Championships since [1932](/wiki/1932_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1932 World Figure Skating Championships").[[2]](#cite_note-host-2) The competition was supposed to determine the entry quotas for each federation at the [2021 World Championships](/wiki/2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 World Figure Skating Championships").

The competition was cancelled on 11 March due to concerns about the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), with the possibility of being held later in the year, but not within the current season.[[3]](#cite_note-CBC-031120-3)[[4]](#cite_note-ISU-031120-4) It was the second time the event had been cancelled for reasons other than a World War, after the 1961 World Championships were cancelled following the [Sabena Flight 548](/wiki/Sabena_Flight_548 "Sabena Flight 548") crash.[[5]](#cite_note-NBC-031120-5)

The competition was formally cancelled on 16 April, after the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU) previously considered rescheduling to later within the year.[[6]](#cite_note-ISU-041620-6) A year later, [Skate Canada](/wiki/Skate_Canada "Skate Canada") successfully bid for the right to host the [2024 World Championships](/wiki/2024_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2024 World Figure Skating Championships") in Montreal in lieu of the cancelled earlier event.[[7]](#cite_note-SC-2024-7)

## Reactions to the COVID-19 pandemic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2020_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Reactions to the COVID-19 pandemic")]

After a spike in [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") cases from the [ongoing pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") and the cancellation of the [2020 Women's Ice Hockey World Championships](/wiki/2020_Women%27s_Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "2020 Women's Ice Hockey World Championships") in [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") by the [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation"), the ISU was placed under intense pressure to make a public statement on the status of the 2020 World Figure Skating Championships, as the disease rapidly intensified across the world. The ISU had implemented prevention methods at its events since 4 February 2020 and required all attendees seeking accreditation to complete a questionnaire and temperature check.[[8]](#cite_note-ISU-030220-8)

[Skate Canada](/wiki/Skate_Canada "Skate Canada"), the host federation, issued a statement on Friday, 6 March, that all athletes would be screened for symptoms at the border, undergo health checks, and be required to fill out questionnaires throughout the competition.[[9]](#cite_note-CBC-030920-9) On Tuesday, 10 March, media, officials, skaters, and coaches scheduled to attend the event received a lengthy Coronavirus Information Package from the ISU. All individuals attending the event would have been required to undergo a temperature check upon arrival to the competition and would have been required to check in under 38 °C (100 °F).[[10]](#cite_note-GN-031020-10) The [Quebec Health Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_and_Social_Services_(Quebec) "Ministry of Health and Social Services (Quebec)") held a press conference the same day to discuss "all major events in the province...with input from the [Public Health Agency of Canada](/wiki/Public_Health_Agency_of_Canada "Public Health Agency of Canada")."[[10]](#cite_note-GN-031020-10) At the meeting, Quebec Minister of Health [Danielle McCann](/wiki/Danielle_McCann "Danielle McCann") indicated that cancellation was a possibility, but a final decision had not yet been reached, despite athletes being scheduled to arrive within the next few days.[[10]](#cite_note-GN-031020-10)

At 3:30 PM EDT on Wednesday, 11 March, the Quebec government and Quebec Health Ministry made the decision to cancel the World Championships.[[3]](#cite_note-CBC-031120-3) The ISU posted a statement agreeing with the decision, saying that the event could possibly be rescheduled for later in the year, but not before October 2020.[[4]](#cite_note-ISU-031120-4) It was unclear how the cancellation would affect the [upcoming season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_figure_skating_season "2020–21 figure skating season") and its subsequent [Grand Prix](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_ISU_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating "2020–21 ISU Grand Prix of Figure Skating") assignments, which will begin before then.

On 12 April 2020, ISU Vice-President for Figure Skating, Alexander Lakernik, told media that the chances of rescheduling the championship were slim, due to the ongoing pandemic.[[11]](#cite_note-RGRU-041220-11) The ISU confirmed a complete cancellation of the event, with no chance of postponement to a later date, on 16 April.[[6]](#cite_note-ISU-041620-6)

### ISU member nations' response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2020_World_Figure_Skating_Championships&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: ISU member nations' response")]

Prior to the cancellation announcement on 11 March, the Polish Figure Skating Association asked its athletes on 10 March to make personal decisions by the next day, 11 March, on whether or not to attend the event, following the [Polish Minister of Sport](/wiki/Ministry_of_Sport_and_Tourism_(Poland) "Ministry of Sport and Tourism (Poland)")'s recommendation to avoid travel to foreign events.[[12]](#cite_note-PFSA-031020-12)

[Skate Canada](/wiki/Skate_Canada "Skate Canada"), the host federation, postponed several conference calls with its skaters from 11 to 13 March, while awaiting a decision by the local Quebec government on the status of the event.[[13]](#cite_note-SC-031120-13)

Several prominent skaters, including two-time reigning men's World Champion [Nathan Chen](/wiki/Nathan_Chen "Nathan Chen"), and coaches [Rafael Arutyunyan](/wiki/Rafael_Arutyunyan "Rafael Arutyunyan"), [Lee Barkell](/wiki/Lee_Barkell "Lee Barkell"), [Marie-France Dubreuil](/wiki/Marie-France_Dubreuil "Marie-France Dubreuil"), and [Brian Orser](/wiki/Brian_Orser "Brian Orser"), expressed concerns over the possibility of rescheduling the competition to fall 2020, citing the disruption to their training schedules and the proximity to the 2021 edition of the event.[[5]](#cite_note-NBC-031120-5)
